revision.text:
{{Otheruses|仏教|地蔵|西念寺|蔦石|柳堂|信州佐久}} '''子育地蔵（佐久市）'''。（こそだてじぞう）は[[長野県佐久市岩村田]]の[[地蔵堂]]の[[地蔵尊]]、[[石仏]]。 == 概略 == 子育地蔵（こそだてじぞう）は、[[長野県佐久市岩村田]]の柳堂地区の地蔵堂に祀られている。 江戸時代に[[宝永山]]が噴火し、その[[降灰]]により飢餓となり、岩村田では３歳以下の子供の[[間引き]](子供狩り)を行い ４０名の[[乳幼児]]が犠牲となったので、その供養のため [[宝永]]5年（[[1707年]]）に建立された。 今も、子供が水疱、風邪、麻疹、おたふく風邪などになった時、お参りすれば、治るとか、子供が丈夫に育つとか、言われ、[[信仰]]されている。 [[昭和]]7年（[[1932年]]）に岩村田蔦石地区から、柳堂地区に遷された。 毎年秋に[[縁日]]を開き、近くの西念寺住職が読経し、多くの子供や崇敬者などが参拝する。 「さらわれし 祖が幼児よ 地蔵尊 果てしなく追う 蔦石の森」の悲しい歌が残っている。 地蔵の台座石は[[岩村田七石]](蔦石、潰石、赤石、夫婦石、信濃石、鞍掛石、勝田石)中の「夫婦石(みょうといし)」の一部。
*ところで、この地蔵より以前にも、蔦石に子育地蔵があったが、安土桃山時代の[[天正年間]]に岩村田住民の多くが[[上州藤岡]]へ移住した時に、この地蔵を背負って運び、藤岡の[[宮本町]]に安置したという。今も宮本町に子育地蔵がある。

{{Otheruses|仏教|地蔵|西念寺|蔦石|柳堂|信州佐久}} '''子育地蔵（佐久市）'''。（こそだてじぞう）は[[長野県佐久市岩村田]]の[[地蔵堂]]の[[地蔵尊]]、[[石仏]]。 == 概略 == 子育地蔵（こそだてじぞう）は、[[長野県佐久市岩村田]]の柳堂地区の地蔵堂に祀られている。 江戸時代に[[宝永山]]が噴火し、その[[降灰]]により飢餓となり、岩村田では３歳以下の子供の[[間引き]](子供狩り)を行い ４０名の[[乳幼児]]が犠牲となったので、その供養のため [[宝永]]5年（[[1707年]]）に建立された。 今も、子供が水疱、風邪、麻疹、おたふく風邪などになった時、お参りすれば、治るとか、子供が丈夫に育つとか、言われ、[[信仰]]されている。 [[昭和]]7年（[[1932年]]）に岩村田蔦石地区から、柳堂地区に遷された。 毎年秋に[[縁日]]を開き、近くの西念寺住職が読経し、多くの子供や崇敬者などが参拝する。 「さらわれし 祖が幼児よ 地蔵尊 果てしなく追う 蔦石の森」の悲しい歌が残っている。 地蔵の台座石は[[岩村田七石]](蔦石、潰石、赤石、夫婦石、信濃石、鞍掛石、勝田石)中の「夫婦石(みょうといし)」の一部<ref>『歴史に学ぶ岩村田の昔佐久市岩村田小学校開校130周年』金沢正昭平成14年11月2日発行全47頁中5頁</ref>。
*ところで、この地蔵より以前にも、蔦石に子育地蔵があったが、安土桃山時代の[[天正年間]]に岩村田住民の多くが[[上州藤岡]]へ移住した時に、この地蔵を背負って運び、藤岡の[[宮本町]]に安置したという。今も宮本町に子育地蔵がある<ref> 『歴史に学ぶ岩村田の昔佐久市岩村田小学校開校130周年』金沢正昭平成14年11月2日発行全47頁中8頁</ref>。

{{Otheruses|仏教|地蔵|西念寺|蔦石|柳堂|信州佐久}} '''子育地蔵'''（こそだてじぞう）は[[長野県佐久市岩村田]]の[[地蔵堂]]の[[地蔵尊]]、[[石仏]]。 == 概略 == 子育地蔵（こそだてじぞう）は、[[長野県佐久市岩村田]]の柳堂地区の地蔵堂に祀られている。 江戸時代に[[宝永山]]が噴火し、その[[降灰]]により飢餓となり、岩村田では３歳以下の子供の[[間引き]](子供狩り)を行い 40名の[[乳幼児]]が犠牲となったので、その供養のため [[宝永]]5年（[[1707年]]）に建立された。 今も、子供が水疱、風邪、麻疹、おたふく風邪などになった時、お参りすれば、治るとか、子供が丈夫に育つとか、言われ、[[信仰]]されている。 [[昭和]]7年（[[1932年]]）に岩村田蔦石地区から、柳堂地区に遷された。 毎年秋に[[縁日]]を開き、近くの西念寺住職が読経し、多くの子供や崇敬者などが参拝する。 「さらわれし 祖が幼児よ 地蔵尊 果てしなく追う 蔦石の森」の悲しい歌が残っている。 地蔵の台座石は[[岩村田七石]](蔦石、潰石、赤石、夫婦石、信濃石、鞍掛石、勝田石)中の「夫婦石(みょうといし)」の一部<ref>『歴史に学ぶ岩村田の昔佐久市岩村田小学校開校130周年』金沢正昭平成14年11月2日発行全47頁中5頁</ref>。
*ところで、この地蔵より以前にも、蔦石に子育地蔵があったが、安土桃山時代の[[天正年間]]に岩村田住民の多くが[[上州藤岡]]へ移住した時に、この地蔵を背負って運び、藤岡の[[宮本町]]に安置したという。今も宮本町に子育地蔵がある<ref> 『歴史に学ぶ岩村田の昔佐久市岩村田小学校開校130周年』金沢正昭平成14年11月2日発行全47頁中8頁</ref>。

{{Otheruses|仏教|地蔵|西念寺|蔦石|柳堂|信州佐久}} '''子育地蔵'''（こそだてじぞう）は、[[長野県]][[佐久市]][[岩村田]]の[[仏道|地蔵堂]]の[[地蔵菩薩|地蔵尊]]、[[石仏]]。
== 概略 == 子育地蔵（こそだてじぞう）は、[[長野県佐久市岩村田]]の柳堂地区の地蔵堂に祀られている。 [[江戸時代]]に[[宝永山]]が[[噴火]]し、その[[降灰]]により[[飢餓]]となり、岩村田では3歳以下の[[子供]]の[[間引き]](子供狩り)を行い 40名の[[乳幼児]]が犠牲となったので、その[[供養]]のため[[宝永]]5年（[[1707年]]）に建立された。 今も、子供が[[水疱]]、[[風邪]]、[[麻疹]]、[[おたふく風邪]]などになった時、お参りすれば、治るとか、子供が丈夫に育つとか、言われ、[[信仰]]されている。 [[昭和]]7年（[[1932年]]）に岩村田蔦石地区から、柳堂地区に遷された。 毎年秋に[[縁日]]を開き、近くの[[西念寺]][[住職]]が[[読経]]し、多くの子供や崇敬者などが参拝する。 「さらわれし 祖が幼児よ 地蔵尊 果てしなく追う 蔦石の森」の悲しい歌が残っている。 地蔵の台座石は[[岩村田七石]](蔦石、潰石、赤石、夫婦石、信濃石、鞍掛石、勝田石)中の「夫婦石(みょうといし)」の一部<ref>『歴史に学ぶ岩村田の昔佐久市岩村田小学校開校130周年』金沢正昭 [[平成]]14年11月2日発行全47頁中5頁</ref>。
*ところで、この地蔵より以前にも、蔦石に子育地蔵があったが、[[安土桃山時代]]の[[天正]]年間に岩村田住民の多くが[[上野国|上州]][[藤岡市|藤岡]]へ移住した時に、この地蔵を背負って運び、藤岡の[[宮本町]]に安置したという。今も宮本町に子育地蔵がある<ref> 『歴史に学ぶ岩村田の昔佐久市岩村田小学校開校130周年』金沢正昭平成14年11月2日発行全47頁中8頁</ref>。

{{Otheruses|仏教|地蔵|西念寺|蔦石|柳堂|信州佐久}} '''子育地蔵'''（こそだてじぞう）は、[[長野県]][[佐久市]][[岩村田]]の[[仏道|地蔵堂]]の[[地蔵菩薩|地蔵尊]]、[[石仏]]。

ところで、この地蔵より以前にも、蔦石に子育地蔵があったが、[[安土桃山時代]]の[[天正]]年間に岩村田住民の多くが[[上野国|上州]][[藤岡市|藤岡]]へ移住した時に、この地蔵を背負って運び、藤岡の[[宮本町]]に安置したという。今も宮本町に子育地蔵がある<ref> 金沢正昭 『歴史に学ぶ岩村田の昔佐久市岩村田小学校開校130周年』 平成14年11月2日発行、全47頁中8頁</ref>。

{{Otheruses|仏教|地蔵|西念寺|蔦石|柳堂|信州佐久}} {{単一の出典|date=2015-10}} '''子育地蔵'''（こそだてじぞう）は、[[長野県]][[佐久市]][[岩村田]]の[[仏道|地蔵堂]]の[[地蔵菩薩|地蔵尊]]、[[石仏]]。
== 概略 == 子育地蔵（こそだてじぞう）は、[[長野県]][[佐久市]]岩村田の柳堂地区の地蔵堂に祀られている。 [[江戸時代]]に[[宝永山]]が[[噴火]]し、その[[降灰]]により[[飢餓]]となり、岩村田では3歳以下の[[子供]]の[[間引き]](子供狩り)を行い 40名の[[乳幼児]]が犠牲となったので、その[[供養]]のため[[宝永]]5年（[[1707年]]）に建立された。 今も、子供が[[水疱]]、[[風邪]]、[[麻疹]]、[[おたふく風邪]]などになった時、お参りすれば、治るとか、子供が丈夫に育つとか、言われ、[[信仰]]されている。 [[昭和]]7年（[[1932年]]）に岩村田蔦石地区から、柳堂地区に遷された。 毎年秋に[[縁日]]を開き、近くの[[西念寺 (佐久市) |西念寺]][[住職]]が[[読経]]し、多くの子供や崇敬者などが参拝する。 「さらわれし 祖が幼児よ 地蔵尊 果てしなく追う 蔦石の森」の悲しい歌が残っている。 地蔵の台座石は[[岩村田七石]](蔦石、潰石、赤石、夫婦石、信濃石、鞍掛石、勝田石)中の「夫婦石(みょうといし)」の一部<ref>金沢正昭 『歴史に学ぶ岩村田の昔佐久市岩村田小学校開校130周年』 平成14年11月2日発行、全47頁中5頁</ref>。
ところで、この地蔵より以前にも、蔦石に子育地蔵があったが、[[安土桃山時代]]の[[天正]]年間に岩村田住民の多くが[[上野国|上州]][[藤岡市|藤岡]]へ移住した時に、この地蔵を背負って運び、藤岡の[[宮本町]]に安置したという。今も宮本町に子育地蔵がある<ref> 金沢正昭 『歴史に学ぶ岩村田の昔佐久市岩村田小学校開校130周年』 平成14年11月2日発行、全47頁中8頁</ref>。

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|仏教|地蔵|西念寺|}} {{単一の出典|date=2015-10}} '''子育地蔵'''（こそだてじぞう）は、[[長野県]][[佐久市]][[岩村田]]の[[仏道|地蔵堂]]の[[地蔵菩薩|地蔵尊]]、[[石仏]]。
== 概略 == 子育地蔵（こそだてじぞう）は、[[長野県]][[佐久市]][[岩村田]]の柳堂地区の地蔵堂に祀られている。 [[江戸時代]]に[[宝永山]]が[[噴火]]し、その降[[灰]]により[[飢餓]]となり、岩村田では3歳以下の[[子供]]の[[間引き]](子供狩り)を行い40名の[[乳幼児]]が犠牲となったので、その[[供養]]のため[[宝永]]5年（[[1707年]]）に建立された<ref>『歴史編中山道岩村田宿』信州佐久いわんだ逸品会平成17年9月26日全29頁中22頁左中図</ref>。 今も、子供が[[水疱]]、[[風邪]]、[[麻疹]]、[[おたふく風邪]]などになった時、お参りすれば、治るとか、子供が丈夫に育つとか、言われ、[[信仰]]されている。 [[昭和]]7年（[[1932年]]）に岩村田蔦石地区から、柳堂地区に遷された。 毎年秋に[[縁日]]を開き、近くの[[西念寺 (佐久市) |西念寺]][[住職]]が[[読経]]し、多くの子供や崇敬者などが参拝する。 「さらわれし 祖が幼児よ 地蔵尊 果てしなく追う 蔦石の森」の悲しい歌が残っている。 地蔵の台座石は岩村田七石(蔦石、潰石、赤石、夫婦石、信濃石、鞍掛石、勝田石)中の「夫婦石(みょうといし)」の一部<ref>金沢正昭 『歴史に学ぶ岩村田の昔佐久市岩村田小学校開校130周年』 平成14年11月2日発行、全47頁中5頁</ref>。
ところで、この地蔵より以前にも、蔦石に子育地蔵があったが、[[安土桃山時代]]の[[天正]]年間に岩村田住民の多くが[[上野国|上州]][[藤岡市|藤岡]]へ移住した時に、この地蔵を背負って運び、藤岡の[[宮本町]]に安置したという<ref>『北佐久口碑伝説集北佐久編限定復刻版』発行者長野県佐久市教育委員会 全434頁中70頁昭和53年11月15日発行</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
{{デフォルトソート:こそたてしそう}} [[Category:佐久市の歴史]] [[Category:地蔵像]]

revision.comment: 『歴史に学ぶ岩村田の昔』出典無効

revision.text:
'''Koyasu Shrine''' (子安神社) is a type of [[Shinto shrine]] primarily dedicated to deities associated with [[safe childbirth]] and [[child-rearing]].
Shrines are generally divided into those enshrining [[Konohanasakuya-hime]], who is also the deity of [[Mount Fuji]] (and the main deity of [[Sengen Shrine]]), and those enshrining [[Kushinadahime]], the wife of [[Susanoo]]. Both deities are genealogically related, with the former being the child and the latter being the grandchild of [[Ōyamatsumi]].
== Kanto Region == * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Mito)|ja|子安神社 (水戸市)}} - Located in Chidami, Mito City, Ibaraki Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Kasumigaura)|ja|子安神社 (かすみがうら市)}} - Located in Higashinoji, Kasumigaura City, Ibaraki Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Tone)|ja|子安神社 (利根町)}} - Located in Obusa, Tone Town, Kita-Soma District, Ibaraki Prefecture. * {{ill|Sakurai Koyasu Shrine|ja|櫻井子安神社}} - Located in Sakurai, Asahi City, Chiba Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Sosa)|ja|子安神社 (匝瑳市)}} - Located in Yokoshiba, Sosa City, Chiba Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Hanamigawa)|ja|子安神社 (千葉市花見川区)}} - Located in Hatamachi, Hanamigawa Ward, Chiba City, Chiba Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Ichikawa)|ja|子安神社 (市川市)}} - Located in Kashiwaimachi, Ichikawa City, Chiba Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Narashino)|ja|子安神社 (習志野市)}} - Located in Fujisaki, Narashino City, Chiba Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Matsudo)|ja|子安神社 (松戸市)}} - Located in Yagasaki, Matsudo City, Chiba Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Hachiman Shrine|ja|子安八幡神社}} - Located in Nakaiikegami and Kitakojiya, Ota Ward, Tokyo. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Hachioji)|ja|子安神社 (八王子市)}} - Located in Hachioji City, Tokyo. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Isehara)|ja|子安神社 (伊勢原市)}} - Located in Ishida, Isehara City, Kanagawa Prefecture.
== Chubu Region == * {{ill|Koyasumiya Shrine|ja|子安宮澤神社}} - Located in Nigioka Town, Otsuki City, Yamanashi Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Hachiman Shrine (Kofu)|ja|子安八幡神社 (甲府市)}} - Located in Kokubo, Kofu City, Yamanashi Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Kawazu)|ja|子安神社 (河津町)}} - Located in Nawa, Kawazu Town, Kamo District, Shizuoka Prefecture. * {{ill|Shimbashi Sengen Shrine|ja|新橋浅間神社}} - Koyasu Shrine as a subordinate shrine of Shimbashi Sengen Shrine, Gotemba City, Shizuoka Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Fujinomiya)|ja|子安神社 (富士宮市)}} - Located in Sugita, Fujinomiya City, Shizuoka Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Hachimangu (Fujinomiya)|ja|子安八幡宮 (富士宮市)}} - Located in Numakubo, Fujinomiya City, Shizuoka Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Hamamatsu)|ja|子安神社 (浜松市東区)}} - Located in Shiratori Town, Hamamatsu City, Shizuoka Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Gifu)|ja|子安神社 (岐阜市)}} - Located in Hikosaka, Gifu City, Gifu Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Ogaki)|ja|子安神社 (大垣市)}} - Located in Akasaka Town, Ogaki City, Gifu Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Obu)|ja|子安神社 (大府市)}} - Located in Kyowa Town, Obu City, Aichi Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Kaga)|ja|子安神社 (加賀市)}} - Located in Kosugawara Town, Kaga City, Ishikawa Prefecture. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Kanazawa)|ja|子安神社 (金沢市)}} - Located in Shimoasahara Town, Kanazawa City, Ishikawa Prefecture.
== Kinki Region == * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Ise)|ja|子安神社 (伊勢市)}} - Located in Ise City, Mie Prefecture, under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮}}. * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Kihoku)|ja|子安神社 (紀宝町)}} - Located in Jinai, Kiho Town, Minami-Muro District, Mie Prefecture.
== Chugoku Region == * {{ill|Koyasu Shrine (Okayama)|ja|子安神社 (岡山市)}} - Located in Kita Ward, Okayama City, Okayama Prefecture. A subordinate shrine of {{ill|Kibitsu Shrine|ja|吉備津彦神社}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Komori Shrine|ja|子守神社}} * {{ill|Kosodate Myōjin|ja|子育明神}} * {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}} ** {{ill|Koyasu Jizō Hall|ja|子安地蔵堂}} ** {{ill|Koyasu Jizō Temple|ja|子安地蔵寺}} * {{ill|Komori Jizō|ja|子守地蔵}} * {{ill|Kosodate Jizō|ja|子育地蔵}} * {{ill|Suitengū|ja|水天宮}}

{{Citation needed|date=2017-06}} [[File:Kosodate-jizo (Saku, Nagano).png|thumb|Kosodate Jizō]] '''Kosodate Jizō''' (子育地蔵) is a [[Jizō Bosatsu|Jizō statue]] (地蔵尊), a form of [[stone Buddha statue]] (石仏), enshrined in a [[Jizō Hall]] (地蔵堂) located in {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}.
== Overview ==
Kosodate Jizō (子育地蔵) is enshrined in the Jizō Hall in the Yanagidō area of {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is still worshiped today, with beliefs that praying to it will cure children of ailments such as [[blisters]] (水疱), [[colds]] (風邪), [[measles]] (麻疹), and [[mumps]] (おたふく風邪), or ensure that they grow up strong and healthy.
In {{ill|Shōwa 7|ja|昭和7年}} ({{ill|1932 AD|ja|1932年}}), the statue was relocated from the Tsuishi area of Iwamurada to the Yanagidō area. Each autumn, a {{ill|Ennichi|ja|縁日}} (festival day) is held, during which the head priest of the nearby {{ill|Sainen-ji (Saku)|ja|西念寺}} chants sutras, and many children and devotees come to pay their respects. A sorrowful poem associated with the statue reads:
*"Sararawareshi / so ga osanago yo / Jizō-son / hateshinaku ou / Tsuishi no mori"*
(*"Carried away / my ancestor's young child / Jizō-son / endlessly pursued / in Tsuishi's forest."*)
The pedestal stone of the Jizō statue is made from one of the "Seven Stones of Iwamurada" (岩村田七石)—specifically, a part of the "Myōto Ishi" (夫婦石, "Husband-and-Wife Stone"). The seven stones include {{ill|Tsuishi|ja|蔦石}}, {{ill|Tsubure Ishi|ja|潰石}}, {{ill|Aka Ishi|ja|赤石}}, {{ill|Myōto Ishi|ja|夫婦石}}, {{ill|Shinano Ishi|ja|信濃石}}, {{ill|Kurakake Ishi|ja|鞍掛石}}, and {{ill|Katsuta Ishi|ja|勝田石}}.
It is said that before the relocation, there was already another Kosodate Jizō in Tsuishi. During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正年間}} of the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代}}, many residents of Iwamurada migrated to {{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|ja|藤岡市}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}. They carried this earlier Jizō statue on their backs and enshrined it in Miyamoto-chō (宮本町) in Fujioka.

(*"Carried away / my ancestor's young child / Jizō-son / endlessly pursued / in Tsuishi's forest."*)
The pedestal stone of the Jizō statue is made from one of the "Seven Stones of Iwamurada" (岩村田七石)—specifically, a part of the "Myōto Ishi" (夫婦石, "Husband-and-Wife Stone"). The seven stones include {{ill|Tsuishi|ja|蔦石}}, {{ill|Tsubure Ishi|ja|潰石}}, {{ill|Aka Ishi|ja|赤石}}, {{ill|Myōto Ishi|ja|夫婦石}}, {{ill|Shinano Ishi|ja|信濃石}}, {{ill|Kurakake Ishi|ja|鞍掛石}}, and {{ill|Katsuta Ishi|ja|勝田石}}.
It is said that before the relocation, there was already another Kosodate Jizō in Tsuishi. During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正年間}} of the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代}}, many residents of Iwamurada migrated to {{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|ja|藤岡市}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}. They carried this earlier Jizō statue on their backs and enshrined it in Miyamoto-chō (宮本町) in Fujioka.

{{Citation needed|date=2017-06}} [[File:Kosodate-jizo (Saku, Nagano).png|thumb|Kosodate Jizō]] '''Kosodate Jizō''' (子育地蔵) is a [[Jizō Bosatsu|Jizō statue]] (地蔵尊), a form of [[stone Buddha statue]] (石仏), enshrined in a [[Jizō Hall]] (地蔵堂) located in {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}.
== Overview ==
Kosodate Jizō (子育地蔵) is enshrined in the Jizō Hall in the Yanagidō area of {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is still worshiped today, with beliefs that praying to it will cure children of ailments such as [[:en:Blisters|blisters]] (水疱), [[colds]] (風邪), [[measles]] (麻疹), and [[mumps]] (おたふく風邪), or ensure that they grow up strong and healthy.
In {{ill|Shōwa 7|ja|昭和7年}} ({{ill|1932 AD|ja|1932年}}), the statue was relocated from the Tsuishi area of Iwamurada to the Yanagidō area. Each autumn, a {{ill|Ennichi|ja|縁日}} (festival day) is held, during which the head priest of the nearby {{ill|Sainen-ji (Saku)|ja|西念寺}} chants sutras, and many children and devotees come to pay their respects. A sorrowful poem associated with the statue reads:
*"Sararawareshi / so ga osanago yo / Jizō-son / hateshinaku ou / Tsuishi no mori"*
(*"Carried away / my ancestor's young child / Jizō-son / endlessly pursued / in Tsuishi's forest."*)
The pedestal stone of the Jizō statue is made from one of the "Seven Stones of Iwamurada" (岩村田七石)—specifically, a part of the "Myōto Ishi" (夫婦石, "Husband-and-Wife Stone"). The seven stones include {{ill|Tsuishi|ja|蔦石}}, {{ill|Tsubure Ishi|ja|潰石}}, {{ill|Aka Ishi|ja|赤石}}, {{ill|Myōto Ishi|ja|夫婦石}}, {{ill|Shinano Ishi|ja|信濃石}}, {{ill|Kurakake Ishi|ja|鞍掛石}}, and {{ill|Katsuta Ishi|ja|勝田石}}.
It is said that before the relocation, there was already another Kosodate Jizō in Tsuishi. During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正年間}} of the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代}}, many residents of Iwamurada migrated to {{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|ja|藤岡市}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}. They carried this earlier Jizō statue on their backs and enshrined it in Miyamoto-chō (宮本町) in Fujioka.

{{Citation needed|date=2017-06}} [[File:Kosodate-jizo (Saku, Nagano).png|thumb|Kosodate Jizō]] '''Kosodate Jizō''' (子育地蔵) is a [[Jizō Bosatsu|Jizō statue]] (地蔵尊), a form of [[stone Buddha statue]] (石仏), enshrined in a [[Jizō Hall]] (地蔵堂) located in {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}.
== Overview ==
Kosodate Jizō (子育地蔵) is enshrined in the Jizō Hall in the Yanagidō area of {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田}}, {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. It is still worshiped today, with beliefs that praying to it will cure children of ailments such as [[:en:Blisters|blisters]] (水疱), [[colds]] (風邪), [[measles]] (麻疹), and [[mumps]] (おたふく風邪), or ensure that they grow up strong and healthy.
In {{ill|Shōwa 7|ja|昭和7th year }} ({{ill|1932 AD|ja|1932nd year }}), the statue was relocated from the Tsuishi area of Iwamurada to the Yanagidō area. Each autumn, a {{ill|Ennichi|ja|縁日}} (festival day) is held, during which the head priest of the nearby {{ill|Sainen-ji (Saku)|ja|西念寺}} chants sutras, and many children and devotees come to pay their respects. A sorrowful poem associated with the statue reads:
*"Sararawareshi / so ga osanago yo / Jizō-son / hateshinaku ou / Tsuishi no mori"*
(*"Carried away / my ancestor's young child / Jizō-son / endlessly pursued / in Tsuishi's forest."*)
The pedestal stone of the Jizō statue is made from one of the "Seven Stones of Iwamurada" (岩村田七石)—specifically, a part of the "Myōto Ishi" (夫婦石, "Husband-and-Wife Stone"). The seven stones include {{ill|Tsuishi|ja|蔦石}}, {{ill|Tsubure Ishi|ja|潰石}}, {{ill|Aka Ishi|ja|赤石}}, {{ill|Myōto Ishi|ja|夫婦石}}, {{ill|Shinano Ishi|ja|信濃石}}, {{ill|Kurakake Ishi|ja|鞍掛石}}, and {{ill|Katsuta Ishi|ja|勝田石}}.
It is said that before the relocation, there was already another Kosodate Jizō in Tsuishi. During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正年間}} of the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代}}, many residents of Iwamurada migrated to {{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|ja|藤岡市}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国}}. They carried this earlier Jizō statue on their backs and enshrined it in Miyamoto-chō (宮本町) in Fujioka.

The pedestal stone of the Jizō statue is made from one of the "Seven Stones of Iwamurada" (岩村田七石)—specifically, a part of the "Myōto Ishi" (夫婦石, "Husband-and-Wife Stone"). The seven stones include {{ill|Tsuishi|ja|蔦石|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsubure Ishi|ja|潰石|12=draft}}, {{ill|Aka Ishi|ja|赤石|12=draft}}, {{ill|Myōto Ishi|ja|夫婦石|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinano Ishi|ja|信濃石|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kurakake Ishi|ja|鞍掛石|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Katsuta Ishi|ja|勝田石|12=draft}}.
It is said that before the relocation, there was already another Kosodate Jizō in Tsuishi. During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正年間|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代|12=draft}}, many residents of Iwamurada migrated to {{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|ja|藤岡市|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国|12=draft}}. They carried this earlier Jizō statue on their backs and enshrined it in Miyamoto-chō (宮本町) in Fujioka.

{{Citation needed|date=2017-06}} [[File:Kosodate-jizo (Saku, Nagano).png|thumb|Kosodate Jizō]] '''Kosodate Jizō''' (子育地蔵) is a [[Jizō Bosatsu|Jizō statue]] (地蔵尊), a form of [[stone Buddha statue]] (石仏), enshrined in a [[Jizō Hall]] (地蔵堂) located in {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}.
== Overview ==
Kosodate Jizō (子育地蔵) is enshrined in the Jizō Hall in the Yanagidō area of {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}. It is still worshiped today, with beliefs that praying to it will cure children of ailments such as [[:en:Blisters|blisters]] (水疱), [[colds]] (風邪), [[measles]] (麻疹), and [[mumps]] (おたふく風邪), or ensure that they grow up strong and healthy.
In {{ill|Shōwa 7|ja|昭和7th year|12=draft}} ({{ill|1932 AD|ja|1932nd year|12=draft}}), the statue was relocated from the Tsuishi area of Iwamurada to the Yanagidō area. Each autumn, a {{ill|Ennichi|ja|縁日|12=draft}} (festival day) is held, during which the head priest of the nearby {{ill|Sainen-ji (Saku)|ja|西念寺|12=draft}} chants sutras, and many children and devotees come to pay their respects. A sorrowful poem associated with the statue reads:
*"Sararawareshi / so ga osanago yo / Jizō-son / hateshinaku ou / Tsuishi no mori"*
(*"Carried away / my ancestor's young child / Jizō-son / endlessly pursued / in Tsuishi's forest."*)
The pedestal stone of the Jizō statue is made from one of the "Seven Stones of Iwamurada" (岩村田七石)—specifically, a part of the "Myōto Ishi" (夫婦石, "Husband-and-Wife Stone"). The seven stones include {{ill|Tsuishi|ja|蔦石|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsubure Ishi|ja|潰石|12=draft}}, {{ill|Aka Ishi|ja|赤石|12=draft}}, {{ill|Myōto Ishi|ja|夫婦石|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shinano Ishi|ja|信濃石|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kurakake Ishi|ja|鞍掛石|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Katsuta Ishi|ja|勝田石|12=draft}}.
It is said that before the relocation, there was already another Kosodate Jizō in Tsuishi. During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正年間|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Azuchi-Momoyama period|ja|安土桃山時代|12=draft}}, many residents of Iwamurada migrated to {{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|ja|藤岡市|12=draft}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|ja|上野国|12=draft}}. They carried this earlier Jizō statue on their backs and enshrined it in Miyamoto-chō (宮本町) in Fujioka.

[[File:Kosodate-jizo (Saku, Nagano).png|thumb|Kosodate Jizō]]
'''Kosodate Jizō''' (子育地蔵) is a [[:en:Jizō Bosatsu|Jizō statue]] (地蔵尊), a form of [[Stone Buddha|Stone Buddha statue]] (石仏), enshrined in a [[Jizō Hall]] (地蔵堂) located in {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|qq=|1=Iwamurada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22127357}}, [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]].

== Overview ==

Kosodate Jizō (子育地蔵) is enshrined in the Jizō Hall in the Yanagidō area of {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|qq=|1=Iwamurada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22127357}}, [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. It is still worshiped today, with beliefs that praying to it will cure children of ailments such as [[:en:Blisters|blisters]] (水疱), [[:en:colds|colds]] (風邪), [[:en:measles|measles]] (麻疹), and [[:en:mumps|mumps]] (おたふく風邪), or ensure that they grow up strong and healthy. 

In [[:en:1932|Showa 7]] ([[:en:1932|1932 AD]]), the statue was relocated from the Tsuishi area of Iwamurada to the Yanagidō area. Each autumn, a [[:en:Ennichi|Ennichi]] (festival day) is held, during which the head priest of the nearby {{ill|Sainen-ji (Saku)|ja|西念寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sainen-ji (Saku)|qq=|1=Sainenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628254}} chants sutras, and many children and devotees come to pay their respects. A sorrowful poem associated with the statue reads:

*"Sararawareshi / so ga osanago yo / Jizō-son / hateshinaku ou / Tsuishi no mori"*

(*"Carried away / my ancestor's young child / Jizō-son / endlessly pursued / in Tsuishi's forest."*)

The pedestal stone of the Jizō statue is made from one of the "Seven Stones of Iwamurada" (岩村田七石)—specifically, a part of the "Myōto Ishi" (夫婦石, "Husband-and-Wife Stone"). The seven stones include {{ill|Tsuishi|ja|蔦石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tsubure Ishi|ja|潰石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubure Ishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Aka Ishi|ja|赤石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aka Ishi|qq=|1=Akaishi, Tahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28057245}}, {{ill|Myōto Ishi|ja|夫婦石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōto Ishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 夫婦岩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 夫婦岩|1=Meoto Iwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333875}}, {{ill|Shinano Ishi|ja|信濃石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kurakake Ishi|ja|鞍掛石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurakake Ishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Katsuta Ishi|ja|勝田石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuta Ishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

It is said that before the relocation, there was already another Kosodate Jizō in Tsuishi. During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正年間|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天正|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天正|1=Tenshō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1145058}} of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama period]], many residents of Iwamurada migrated to [[:en:Fujioka, Gunma|Fujioka, Gunma]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. They carried this earlier Jizō statue on their backs and enshrined it in Miyamoto-chō (宮本町) in Fujioka.

'''Kosodate Jizō''' (子育地蔵) is a [[:en:Jizō Bosatsu|Jizō statue]] (地蔵尊), a form of [[Stone Buddha|Stone Buddha statue]] (石仏), enshrined in a [[Jizō Hall]] (地蔵堂) located in {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|qq=|1=Iwamurada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22127357}}, [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]].

== Overview ==

Kosodate Jizō (子育地蔵) is enshrined in the Jizō Hall in the Yanagidō area of {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|qq=|1=Iwamurada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22127357}}, [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. It is still worshiped today, with beliefs that praying to it will cure children of ailments such as [[:en:Blisters|blisters]] (水疱), [[:en:colds|colds]] (風邪), [[:en:measles|measles]] (麻疹), and [[:en:mumps|mumps]] (おたふく風邪), or ensure that they grow up strong and healthy. 

In [[:en:1932|Showa 7]] ([[:en:1932|1932 AD]]), the statue was relocated from the Tsuishi area of Iwamurada to the Yanagidō area. Each autumn, a [[:en:Ennichi|Ennichi]] (festival day) is held, during which the head priest of the nearby {{ill|Sainen-ji (Saku)|ja|西念寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sainen-ji (Saku)|qq=|1=Sainenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628254}} chants sutras, and many children and devotees come to pay their respects. A sorrowful poem associated with the statue reads:

*"Sararawareshi / so ga osanago yo / Jizō-son / hateshinaku ou / Tsuishi no mori"*

(*"Carried away / my ancestor's young child / Jizō-son / endlessly pursued / in Tsuishi's forest."*)

The pedestal stone of the Jizō statue is made from one of the "Seven Stones of Iwamurada" (岩村田七石)—specifically, a part of the "Myōto Ishi" (夫婦石, "Husband-and-Wife Stone"). The seven stones include {{ill|Tsuishi|ja|蔦石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tsubure Ishi|ja|潰石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubure Ishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Aka Ishi|ja|赤石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aka Ishi|qq=|1=Akaishi, Tahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28057245}}, {{ill|Myōto Ishi|ja|夫婦石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōto Ishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 夫婦岩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 夫婦岩|1=Meoto Iwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333875}}, {{ill|Shinano Ishi|ja|信濃石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kurakake Ishi|ja|鞍掛石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurakake Ishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Katsuta Ishi|ja|勝田石|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuta Ishi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

It is said that before the relocation, there was already another Kosodate Jizō in Tsuishi. During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正年間|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō era|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天正|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天正|1=Tenshō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1145058}} of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama period]], many residents of Iwamurada migrated to [[:en:Fujioka, Gunma|Fujioka, Gunma]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. They carried this earlier Jizō statue on their backs and enshrined it in Miyamoto-chō (宮本町) in Fujioka.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:子育地蔵]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q22124254]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:39, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q22124254]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=2017-06}} [[File:Kosodate-jizo (Saku, Nagano).png|thumb|Kosodate Jizō]] '''Kosodate Jizō''' (子育地蔵) is a [[:en:Jizō Bosatsu|Jizō statue]] (地蔵尊), a form of [[Stone Buddha|Stone Buddha statue]] (石仏), enshrined in a [[Jizō Hall]] (地蔵堂) located in {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|1=Iwamurada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22127357}}, [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]].
== Overview ==
Kosodate Jizō (子育地蔵) is enshrined in the Jizō Hall in the Yanagidō area of {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|1=Iwamurada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22127357}}, [[:en:Saku, Nagano|Saku, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. It is still worshiped today, with beliefs that praying to it will cure children of ailments such as [[:en:Blisters|blisters]] (水疱), [[:en:colds|colds]] (風邪), [[:en:measles|measles]] (麻疹), and [[:en:mumps|mumps]] (おたふく風邪), or ensure that they grow up strong and healthy.
In [[:en:1932|Showa 7]] ([[:en:1932|1932 AD]]), the statue was relocated from the Tsuishi area of Iwamurada to the Yanagidō area. Each autumn, a [[:en:Ennichi|Ennichi]] (festival day) is held, during which the head priest of the nearby {{ill|Sainen-ji (Saku)|ja|西念寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sainen-ji (Saku)|1=Sainenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628254}} chants sutras, and many children and devotees come to pay their respects. A sorrowful poem associated with the statue reads:
*"Sararawareshi / so ga osanago yo / Jizō-son / hateshinaku ou / Tsuishi no mori"*
(*"Carried away / my ancestor's young child / Jizō-son / endlessly pursued / in Tsuishi's forest."*)
The pedestal stone of the Jizō statue is made from one of the "Seven Stones of Iwamurada" (岩村田七石)—specifically, a part of the "Myōto Ishi" (夫婦石, "Husband-and-Wife Stone"). The seven stones include {{ill|Tsuishi|ja|蔦石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tsubure Ishi|ja|潰石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubure Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Aka Ishi|ja|赤石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aka Ishi|1=Akaishi, Tahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28057245}}, {{ill|Myōto Ishi|ja|夫婦石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōto Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 夫婦岩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 夫婦岩|1=Meoto Iwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333875}}, {{ill|Shinano Ishi|ja|信濃石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kurakake Ishi|ja|鞍掛石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurakake Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Katsuta Ishi|ja|勝田石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuta Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
It is said that before the relocation, there was already another Kosodate Jizō in Tsuishi. During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正年間|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天正|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天正|1=Tenshō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1145058}} of the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama period]], many residents of Iwamurada migrated to [[:en:Fujioka, Gunma|Fujioka, Gunma]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. They carried this earlier Jizō statue on their backs and enshrined it in Miyamoto-chō (宮本町) in Fujioka.

(*"Carried away / my ancestor's young child / Jizō-son / endlessly pursued / in Tsuishi's forest."*)

The seven stones include {{ill|Tsuishi|ja|蔦石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tsubure Ishi|ja|潰石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubure Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Aka Ishi|ja|赤石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aka Ishi|1=Akaishi, Tahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28057245}}, {{ill|Myōto Ishi|ja|夫婦石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōto Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 夫婦岩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 夫婦岩|1=Meoto Iwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333875}}, {{ill|Shinano Ishi|ja|信濃石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kurakake Ishi|ja|鞍掛石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurakake Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Katsuta Ishi|ja|勝田石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuta Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
It is said that before the relocation, there was already another Kosodate Jizō in Tsuishi. During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正年間|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天正|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天正|1=Tenshō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1145058}} of the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama period}}, many residents of Iwamurada migrated to {{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|en|Fujioka, Gunma|WD=Q596486|lt=Fujioka, Gunma}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}. They carried this earlier Jizō statue on their backs and enshrined it in Miyamoto-chō (宮本町) in Fujioka.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:子育地蔵]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:子育地蔵]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:子育地蔵]]
{{Citation needed|date=2017-06}} [[File:Kosodate-jizo (Saku, Nagano).png|thumb|Kosodate Jizō]] '''Kosodate Jizō''' (子育地蔵) is a [[:en:Jizō Bosatsu|Jizō statue]] (地蔵尊), a form of [[Stone Buddha|Stone Buddha statue]] (石仏), enshrined in a [[Jizō Hall]] (地蔵堂) located in {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|1=Iwamurada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22127357}}, {{ill|Saku, Nagano|en|Saku, Nagano|WD=Q495821|lt=Saku, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}.
== Overview ==
Kosodate Jizō (子育地蔵) is enshrined in the Jizō Hall in the Yanagidō area of {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|1=Iwamurada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22127357}}, {{ill|Saku, Nagano|en|Saku, Nagano|WD=Q495821|lt=Saku, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}. It is still worshiped today, with beliefs that praying to it will cure children of ailments such as [[:en:Blisters|blisters]] (水疱), [[:en:colds|colds]] (風邪), {{ill|measles|en|measles|WD=Q79793|lt=measles}} (麻疹), and {{ill|mumps|en|mumps|WD=Q176741|lt=mumps}} (おたふく風邪), or ensure that they grow up strong and healthy.
In {{ill|1932|en|1932|WD=Q18743|lt=Showa 7}} ({{ill|1932|en|1932|WD=Q18743|lt=1932 AD}}), the statue was relocated from the Tsuishi area of Iwamurada to the Yanagidō area. Each autumn, a {{ill|Ennichi|en|Ennichi|WD=Q985655|lt=Ennichi}} (festival day) is held, during which the head priest of the nearby {{ill|Sainen-ji (Saku)|ja|西念寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sainen-ji (Saku)|1=Sainenji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628254}} chants sutras, and many children and devotees come to pay their respects. A sorrowful poem associated with the statue reads:
*"Sararawareshi / so ga osanago yo / Jizō-son / hateshinaku ou / Tsuishi no mori"*
(*"Carried away / my ancestor's young child / Jizō-son / endlessly pursued / in Tsuishi's forest."*)
The pedestal stone of the Jizō statue is made from one of the "Seven Stones of Iwamurada" (岩村田七石)—specifically, a part of the "Myōto Ishi" (夫婦石, "Husband-and-Wife Stone"). The seven stones include {{ill|Tsuishi|ja|蔦石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tsubure Ishi|ja|潰石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubure Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Aka Ishi|ja|赤石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aka Ishi|1=Akaishi, Tahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28057245}}, {{ill|Myōto Ishi|ja|夫婦石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōto Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 夫婦岩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 夫婦岩|1=Meoto Iwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333875}}, {{ill|Shinano Ishi|ja|信濃石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kurakake Ishi|ja|鞍掛石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurakake Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Katsuta Ishi|ja|勝田石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuta Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
It is said that before the relocation, there was already another Kosodate Jizō in Tsuishi. During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正年間|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天正|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天正|1=Tenshō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1145058}} of the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama period}}, many residents of Iwamurada migrated to {{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|en|Fujioka, Gunma|WD=Q596486|lt=Fujioka, Gunma}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}. They carried this earlier Jizō statue on their backs and enshrined it in Miyamoto-chō (宮本町) in Fujioka.

The seven stones include {{ill|Tsuishi|ja|蔦石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tsubure Ishi|ja|潰石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsubure Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Aka Ishi|ja|赤石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aka Ishi|1=Akaishi, Tahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28057245}}, {{ill|Myōto Ishi|ja|夫婦石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōto Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 夫婦岩|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 夫婦岩|1=Meoto Iwa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q333875}}, {{ill|Shinano Ishi|ja|信濃石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinano Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Kurakake Ishi|ja|鞍掛石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurakake Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Katsuta Ishi|ja|勝田石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuta Ishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
It is said that before the relocation, there was already another Kosodate Jizō in Tsuishi. During the {{ill|Tenshō era|ja|天正年間|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō era|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天正|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1145058}} of the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi-Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, many residents of Iwamurada migrated to {{ill|Fujioka, Gunma|en|Fujioka, Gunma|WD=Q596486|lt=Fujioka, Gunma|1=Fujioka, Gunma}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province|1=Kōzuke Province}}. They carried this earlier Jizō statue on their backs and enshrined it in Miyamoto-chō (宮本町) in Fujioka.

Kosodate Jizō (子育地蔵) is enshrined in the Jizō Hall in the Yanagidō area of {{ill|Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|ja|岩村田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamurada, Saku, Nagano|1=Iwamurada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q22127357}}, {{ill|Saku, Nagano|en|Saku, Nagano|WD=Q495821|lt=Saku, Nagano|1=Saku, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}.
